steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The assertion says flies from warmer months should have larger body sizes, which correlates with longer life spans. To complete the assertion, we need data showing a warmer month with larger wing centroid size than a cooler month — both a temperature difference and a corresponding wing size difference.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'the average male wing centroid size was larger in July than in May': July is warmer than May, and July males had larger wing centroid size (2.02 mm vs. 1.98 mm) — directly confirms the warmer-month/larger-body prediction. ✓
  • Choice B — 'average female wing centroid size was consistently larger than male in all four months': compares sexes, not seasons — irrelevant to the warm-month prediction. ✗
  • Choice C — 'average monthly low temperature was higher in September than in May': only compares temperatures, includes no body size data at all. ✗
  • Choice D — 'average female wing centroid size was 2.02 mm in July but 2.29 mm in October': October is cooler than July, yet the size is larger — this contradicts, not supports, the assertion. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
